speaker: interviewer
text: --with Elly Gross. Mrs. Gross, please tell us your number that you received at Auschwitz.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm going back where I was. All the [? Salajans, ?] they called the [ROMANIAN]-- [ROMANIAN]. And from the city of Simleu, the first group , we were taken to the block 20 where it was raining all night.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right

speaker: interviewee
text: And since the-- even the Germans, they know they cannot keep us in the water. We were standing under knee-high water. The other blocks, there were 32 in that [GERMAN]. It was told to huge blockalteste they have to come to take from the blocks 20 people into their block. And I was lucky that my-- I should say, not lucky, but also a luck-- my cousins, they were on the block 18th. They are from Bihor, Judetul Bihor, Marghita. They were in Laichman, Miri's, block. She was the rabbi's daughter from Bratislava. She was the blockalteste. And she came with people from her block to take from the block 20, which we were from Salaj, to her block, about 100 to 200 persons. And my two cousins-- one of my cousins-- there are two cousins, Eva and Yboia Farkas-- they were on the block 18, which was the block mainly from the Bihor, Judetul Bihor. They came with the blockalteste, Miri. And they called my mother and my name. My mother wasn't there. I was there, and then I was taken with them to the block 18.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they actually know you were there, or they were just trying to find out if you-- if you were there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nobody know who is there. Just-- she was just trying to bring-- the Miri, this Miri was very nice. She wanted to bring the families close together.

speaker: interviewer
text: So she took in her block. The others, I don't know what they did. But she wanted to try to bring the families together. That's the reason she took on her block.

speaker: interviewee
text: And just they were-- they didn't know who was there. Just they came. So I was moved to the block 18. There was a big difference from the 20. Here there were three beds on top of each other, 14 on each bed. And when the-- somebody was sitting, and they was too then we couldn't straight her neck. [BACKGROUND NOISE] When they try-- when we were trying to sleep, if one was turning, the other one had to turn too. Because they were so tight, the bed. I don't know how big it was, but it was very small.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many were actually on a bed?

speaker: interviewee
text: 14. And 14 on each row. But the-- the-- the whole block was full. And they were-- we were about 1,000 or 1,500 in that little one block, block 18. On the-- we had to get up early in the morning, 3:00 about, had to be. Because the star was still up. It was very chilly. And had to wait there for the-- [? say ?] appell. You know, they were coming, always counting us, the Germans. And afternoon, right after noon, right again was the second [? say ?] appell until late at night. The third-- the second or the third, they were always exhausting. And everything, what happened to me, I just passed out. And since I was-- I guess my face was a young kid, Miri took me to clean the chimney. It was in each block had a long chimney with two big horns on each end. And in the middle, a flat, low-- they're low, about one meter high and a half a meter wide-- connection. And my job was to get a brick to scrub all day, the brick-- the other brick with a broken brick to be nice and red in between the brick. The cement with a Clorox paste, with my finger, I was pasting to look white. And because I did that, I didn't have to go out to do when was every day the selection. They-- Mengele and Margot [? Grazer ?] were selecting 50, 60, 100 person for their-- I know now for what. At the time, I didn't. For the experiment, what Germans, they did. And with that, that Miri-- took me to clean that chimney, I was saved. Because I was extremely white, blonde, and blue eyes. Probably, I would have been the first who Mengele would select. Sometimes-- I know now. At the time, I didn't-- the blood was taken out from the selected people. And when they couldn't move, they didn't have any more blood, they were just thrown to the [? crematorium, ?] just was left to die that way. [SIGHS] I was doing that every single day. Because I did that, I didn't have to go out, 3:00 in the morning, when everybody was always started to work. And I just had to go out in my line, where there was five of us, my two cousins and their two cousins. Which happened to be, later on, my two sister, older sister. One of the sister is still alive, is about my mother's age in Israel.

speaker: interviewer
text: May I ask you something? Did you start to menstruate before you got to--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: --Auschwitz?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I did. Because I was very tall. I didn't grow since then. Since-- actually, I shrink already. And I was about 12, maybe only 11 and a 1/2. And as soon I drink that three-- swallow-- I swallowed the three swallowing food, stop my menstruation. I-- I-- exactly I had, at the time. And I think-- I don't know what was in the food, but somehow made us not to think anymore. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you think it was the food, or do you think it was the stress?

speaker: interviewee
text: What? No, the food-- it was in the food, something. Was a bitter something, a bitter-- if I remember, even now, I remember it was a bitter taste. Had a very bitter taste. But we were so hungry, exhausted, thirsty. And even if we knew what is in the food, we would drink it. Because even if there would be poison, we wouldn't think about not to drink. Because we were just-- it was the edge. We were at the edge. As I said, the third day I passed out on the floor, on the-- on the-- where we were standing on appell. So Miri, with that, that she selected me to do the chimneys, to scrub the chimney, she saved my life. Because I would have been the first who-- who would have been selected. While we were there, everybody was thirsty. Now our-- to keep in order this huge amount of woman was a strict lager law. No one was allowed to walk. If somebody had to go to the bathroom, you had to-- couldn't go to the bathroom. In the back was a-- a bucket. Water was not allowed, no one to drink it. Because then you had to go, right away, to the bathroom. And but I was such a good kid all my life. And I liked to help. And no one asked me ever to do it, but I did it. I was carrying, all day long, the water for the people, for everyone who said they're thirsty, with a big [? bucket ?], I was carrying with a big pot to each bed, the water. There was, as I said, three-- three on top of each other.

speaker: interviewer
text: You mean at night?

speaker: interviewee
text: During the day.

speaker: interviewer
text: What-- what were these other women doing during the day?

speaker: interviewee
text: Just sitting. Sitting or lying on the bed, some sitting. They were not-- no one-- no one was allowed to walk. And one day, on the next lager, they were brought in. Some looked skeletons. People we know because Miri was from Czechoslovakia. I mean, from Slovakia, she knew the Czech-- the Czech Jews from Theresienstadt, they looked skeletons. They had clothing. But the children, they looked like-- I don't know-- they looked like monkeys. They didn't look like humans. They were only bones. And they were very, very, very sick. Every single day, a cart with-- like logs, two rows of dead people were taken away from there. We saw them, because sometimes we were sent to bring the food. And where we went to bring the food with a big, big-- we carried on our hand, and we saw these people. And when we-- whoever-- who went to for food was very lucky. Because if you went for food, you got a little potato peel. And that was-- you saved your life. And then when I got a little potato peel, I took to my cousins. And we divide it. And it was given us, bread. And I was very trusting all the time. And I never even thought about-- some people, they were fighting for bread. Always, when I got my portion of bread, I gave to my older cousin, Yboia. And she was slicing for us. And she-- she saved for us, all the time, another portion for the next day, just in case we don't get. And I never looked-- never thought about God forbid, I should look at that. Maybe the slice, what she's giving me, are eventually smaller. I was you know, just-- I trusted, like I would have done. I never even thought about something like that. When I got a little potato peel if I brought the food, I gave to her. She divided between us. And as I mentioned, and I thought these Czechoslovakian Jews which was brought from Theresienstadt. And one day, we had a big-- they were very, very sick. I heard they have typhus. But it's-- they were out of their-- starved for years. They-- they were just skeleton. And one night, we had a very strict day-and-night lager curfew-- curfew, that no one was allowed to go out. Every block was locked. And just the Czechoslovakian disappeared. I saw Miri, our blockalteste. For days, she was just crying. I didn't know at the time, but I know that was the liquidation of the real-- the leftover Jews from Theresienstadt. Now I know. At the time, I didn't. And the-- every day, they were selecting, as I said before. I never been out when they were selecting, just a few. On the end of July-- not July, August, they were-- they were starting to-- I didn't know at the time. Now I know-- liquidating Auschwitz. Because probably the front was coming close. And we were selected to go to work. So Miri said to everyone who wants, to go out from Auschwitz is better. We-- oh, I skipped something. One day, Miri's fiance-- was working in the crematorium. He was coming, now and then, to see her. And Dr. Nyiszli-- Nyiszli, Miklos, from Oradea. And his wife and his daughter was in my barrack, in barrack 18. Block 18.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did this happen? They were not so segregated, or some people had privileges?

speaker: interviewee
text: The-- these blockaltestes, they had some knowledge of each other. But Dr. Nyiszli, we didn't know where he is working. Now I know he was Mengele's assistant. He came to see his wife. He brought some aspirin with him, some food. And he told to go-- his wife and daughter, as soon as possible, they should go away from Auschwitz, if it's possible. And Miri told us, who-- better for us to go to work. And we went to selection. The selection was-- oh, Miri, one day was taken to-- not only Miri-- every blockalteste from lager C-- the C lager was taken to work on the road. And on that day, the crematorium people was liquidated. I didn't know at the time, but Miri, she was crying desperately for many, many days. She knew it. She never told us. Since I read, after the liberation, many books, I figured out myself at that day was her fiance liquidated.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was that the-- the uprising of the--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, it was before. Because every three, four months, they were changed.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah, and they--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. The uprising was later on. We weren't there at that time, in Auschwitz. When Miri came back from that working day-- she was work on the field. I mean, the-- on the road-- she was very exhausted. And a lady on that day, had a diarrhea, from Marghita, Mrs. Goldberg. And one of the girls who was in charge, she told Miri that Mrs. Goldberg went to the-- to the back more than one time. She had to go. And Miri came. When she returned, she beat her with a stick 10 times. And that was very sad, very sad. She was such a nice person. But I have to understand that she was exhausted herself, too.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did she survive the war, this lady?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. She went to Israel. And everybody loved her. She was a very nice person. She tried to save us, and helped us. When I-- When the selection came for the work, we went through naked from the block 18 to the 16. Because the-- the even sizes-- numbers, they were one side. And the uneven, the odd numbers on the other side. And the 16 was next to us. And I remember standing in front of Mengele and a Czechoslovakian tall lady. She-- I was tall enough. But she was taller than me. And I was selected, between the others, to go to work. We were taken to disinfection, to the bath. And over there were giving us to cloth-- clothing. When we were ready to go on the trains, something had to happen. The train or the factory had to be destroyed or something. I don't know what happened. Because we didn't go to the work. We were taken to the lager B, which was cross from the C. And over there was so friendly-- I thought so-- because was no fence, no electrical wire, no-- no guard in the corners. It was so nice. And the-- the-- instead of the fence, wire fence, they were-- fence-- the fence covered with blankets. And be-- behind the blankets was some smoke coming from the ground. Now I know what it was. There was the pit where they burned the bodies. The crematorium couldn't take it. There were too many. The--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you smell something terrible?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, of course sadly, was a smell like a burned rubber. I thought it's a rubber factory. And actually, I thought they're going to take us to work in the factory. All the time, I thought that. In the beginning, the first time too. When we get off from the train. I mentioned briefly--

speaker: interviewer
text: Mhm.

speaker: interviewee
text: --that was music there, like symphony music. It was so nice, friendly. Just 'Arbeit macht frei' the-- on the gates. So naturally, we thought that-- I thought we're going to work. In the B lager-- [SIGHS] --we had to take off every cloth again. We were naked on the-- all day in the yard, at night-- and no food. At night, we were-- they told us to go into the barracks. And when we walked in, everybody got a pajama top. Somehow, I got a pants. And because I got a pants, I-- somebody hit me, that I broke my teeth-- one of my front teeth. That wasn't a German. It was a-- a Polish-Jewish person. And in the-- we slept on the floor. The barrack was pretty clean. And the next day, we were taken again to disinfection. And we were given us clothes. And we were-- we were taken to the wagons, to train. When I walked up on the train, I was on the first. My cousins, they were cut off. So I was then separated of them. I thought they are going to be on the next train. When we walked out in Fallersleben, I didn't see them. We were 500. And we were placed in the-- across the street from the factory, like a-- a semi-basement, a big place. And because here we had cold and hot water and clean floor, and we got, every day, a big plate of beets to eat. In the morning, we had a piece of bread and a certain kind of bitter coffee. And at night, and again some kind of soup, beet soup. It was a big change from Auschwitz. And we were selected. I mean, we were sent to work. I was selected to work between 10 of-- I mean, 10 of us, we were put to work in-- in the painting quarter. [BACKGROUND NOISE] And over there, I got a-- like a pistol, where I had to push the pin to come out. And the automatically, the piece was turning. And the Germans, they gave us who worked there a mask, a robe-- a half a robe and a glove, and a 1/2 a liter skim milk every day, who worked on that place.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were you making there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Painting.

speaker: interviewer
text: But what were you painting?

speaker: interviewee
text: I said, it's a round thing, it was turning. I think that was the satellite, or missiles, whatever they used, the Germans, with no man to bomb London and other places. I think they were the first who used and invented the missiles. No-man missiles.

speaker: interviewer
text: No it was-- it-- it was part of--

speaker: interviewee
text: It's part of that missiles. In a certain place, was not allowed to let paint to go. And every day, three, four person was checking us all the time, that the paint shouldn't go there. The piece, what we painted, went on a chain to the dryer. And was something wrong, they brought it back. They put it back to wash. And then after, to paint again. Because constantly, they were checking. We had two-- three meisters over there. One, a Czechoslovakian, one, a Ukrainian, and one, a German. The-- as soon I worked over there and a few weeks after, my mouth started to bleed. And I started to cough. Probably the mask wasn't good. And the paint, I don't know, if it get into my mouth or something. I don't know why. And the German meister, he brought for me a little piece, about a half a glass of salt to rinse my mouth. If they would caught him, I'm sure he would be severely punished. He said, the [GERMAN], he said the [GERMAN]. I don't remember exactly. I don't know now, already, German. And I rinsed my mouth. And I got a little bit better. But my mouth was constantly bleeding. And the cough finally got so severe that I had fever. I felt chilly-- a chill over my body. And whoever got sick on that place was taken away, and no one ever heard of it. I got very, very sick. I had fever. I had-- we had a tech-- dental technician. And she told me I have 38 1/2, she measured. But I had to go to work. But sometimes, just I couldn't anymore. Because I was so shivering, and trembling, and coughing so terribly, that no one was able to sleep in the whole room. Because I was coughing. I kept my head on the side of the bed. I was sleeping on the top of the bed, because it was easier for me to cough. And no one was allowed to work with a blanket or to get sick. As I said, the sick person was taken away. And I put a blanket on me. And I went to the lady. And I said, I cannot go to work. I was working, I don't know, somehow-- never changed. I have to work always, at night, 12 hours. I was on that shift. And during the day, was always a air raid. We had to go to the bunker. So even if I would be able to sleep, I never was able to sleep. Because I was just coughing. When on the weekend we didn't work, I woke up everybody, because I was coughing so horribly. Or sometimes I think was changed, the shift. Because sometimes I slept-- I would have slept all night if I would-- if I wouldn't cough. I took the blanket, and I went to her. And the [GERMAN] was coming. And it was-- he had like a club stick with him. And he-- sometimes he beat people. And everybody just hold their breath if they was thinking that I'm going to be beaten. He didn't beat me. And asked me, [SPEAKING GERMAN]. And I said, yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: So he told the blockalteste that she should put me to clean inside the place, to wash the floor and whatever has to be done inside. And I didn't go-- I didn't have to go to work to the factory.

speaker: interviewee
text: Somehow I think that helped me. It was already already, I think, March or something, of 1945. On the beginning or in the middle of April, we were evacuated. We're taken to the trains.

speaker: interviewer
text: About how many of you were there by that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: By that time-- we started 500. A few were taken away because they were sick. And between that time, could you mention, 50 Hollanders, they were brought over there to increase our numbers, and 50 Yugoslavian partisans. But all of us--

speaker: interviewer
text: Women, these were all women?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were all woman, we were. Above us, there were a few German ladies. I know, even, their name-- [? Kristina, ?] [? Anna Maria, ?] and one was [? Paulina. ?] And they-- I didn't have, with no one, problems. Because I just worked until I was able. And when I-- they put me to clean, I was cleaning. And-- in the beginning-- about in the middle of April, 1945, we were evacuated with all the newcomers and whoever left from between us. Because as I said, a few were taken away because they got sick. And nobody ever heard of them. And when-- finally, when we were taken-- we-- I don't know how long we were in the-- in the cattle wagon, we arrived to Salzwedel. Over there I met, again, my cousins from I was separated in August, 1944. And two, three weeks, May 8th, the end-- the last day of the war, we were liberated by the American Army.

speaker: interviewer
text: What-- what-- what were you doing in Salzwedel?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nothing. We were doing nothing. We were just--

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you fed, somewhat?

speaker: interviewee
text: We got some kind of food. But I don't-- briefly, I don't really remember exactly. We got--

speaker: interviewer
text: It was nothing-- nothing too much one way or the other?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, nothing. What wasn't so major thing, that--

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you aware?

speaker: interviewee
text: Maybe we were only-- only one week. I don't even remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you aware that perhaps the war was going to be over soon?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, we didn't know nothing.

speaker: interviewer
text: You didn't? Did you have any sense that--

speaker: interviewee
text: Nothing, we had nothing. We don't know nothing. We didn't know nothing. Especially I didn't know nothing. Maybe the older one, they knew something. But no one ever told me anything. No one ever wanted to talk with a kid. I was at that time, already 16. But I was still a child. And especially with this hard time that I grew up, I remained somehow-- I was an early adult and I remained a child. I never grew up. When we were liberated, I saw a German was shot over there at the front. I was taken away after very fast. And everyone was given free day to go out from the lager.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where--

speaker: interviewee
text: In Salzwedel.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was this?

speaker: interviewee
text: Salzwedel.

speaker: interviewer
text: Salzwedel. Where-- where in Germany, do you know where it was?

speaker: interviewee
text: I know on-- on the-- I know on the map--

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah?

speaker: interviewee
text: --where it is, because I looked it up, Salzwedel.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: But I don't know exactly, now, where it was. I know that Fallersleben was 60 kilometer from Hannover, which was a major city. But Salzwedel, couldn't be too far, because--

speaker: interviewer
text: How long did it take you to get there?

speaker: interviewee
text: One day and a night over there, via the train.

speaker: interviewer
text: You walked? You walked or you were on the train?

speaker: interviewee
text: On the train.

speaker: interviewer
text: On the train.

speaker: interviewee
text: But always, the train stop. Because for one reason or the other. Who know exactly? We were locked in the train. We were given a piece of bread and that's all. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: You were liberated by whom?

speaker: interviewee
text: American Army. By the American Army on May 8th, the last day of the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you very much.

speaker: interviewee
text: The tape is finished again.
